title: Placenta Praevia
oneLiner: Placenta partially or completely overlies internal cervical os after 20 weeks; painless bleeding in third trimester is the classic presentation; risk of catastrophic maternal haemorrhage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive antepartum haemorrhage (APH) with shock:** The bleeding is unprovoked, bright red, and painless. The single finding: **tachycardia >110 bpm with postural hypotension** despite a "small" amount of visible blood — the concealed haemorrhage can be larger than it appears.
- **Vasa praevia:** Fetal vessels running across the membranes over the os. Presents with sudden, painless bleeding *and* **fetal bradycardia <100 bpm** — the baby bleeds from its own circulation. A normal maternal pulse and blood pressure with a distressed fetus should raise this immediately.
- **Placental abruption with concealed haemorrhage:** Painful, hard "woody" uterus, often with fetal distress. **Uterine hypertonus on palpation** differentiates it from praevia (soft uterus). If you see a rigid uterus + fetal bradycardia, think abruption first.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - **Painless, bright red vaginal bleeding** (classic – 90% of cases) — usually after 28 weeks, may be recurrent or single episode
  - Sometimes only spotting or brown discharge (low-grade bleed)
  - May be provoked by sexual intercourse or vaginal exam (massively increases bleeding risk)
  - Bleeding can be torrential or intermittent
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Uterus is **soft and non-tender** (differentiates from abruption)
  - Fetal lie often **breech or transverse** due to placenta occupying lower segment
  - If bleeding heavily: maternal tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, clammy skin
  - **Avoid digital vaginal exam** – this can precipitate catastrophic haemorrhage; only speculum exam under ultrasound guidance after praevia excluded
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Placental abruption** — painful, hard uterus, concealed bleed, fetal distress out of proportion to visible blood
- **Vasa praevia** — painless bleeding + fetal bradycardia, maternal vitals stable; diagnose with colour Doppler ultrasound
- **Cervical/ectropion** — bleeding after intercourse/exam, no fetal distress, ultrasound normal
- **Cervical/vulval trauma** — history of trauma, localised bleeding
- **Preterm labour** — contractions with show, not pure fresh bleeding
- **Lower genital tract infection** — discharge with blood streaks, not heavy
- **Splitter:** **Pain on palpation of the uterus** → abruption (hard, tender) vs praevia (soft, non-tender) — this is the most reliable bedside differentiator.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Fetal heart auscultation** or **CTG** — look for bradycardia, decelerations (fetal distress)
  - **Speculum exam** (gentle) — visualise cervical os: is bleeding coming through os? Avoid digital exam.
  - **Ultrasound (urgent):** confirm placental location, measure distance from internal os; transvaginal is safe and accurate (no harm distal to os)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, platelets), group & screen (cross-match if bleeding)
  - Clotting screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen) — DIC can develop rapidly with large bleed
  - Kleihauer test (if Rh-negative, to calculate anti-D dose)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transabdominal ultrasound (screening); if equivocal, transvaginal (gold standard)
  - **Second-line:** MRI if suspected placenta accreta (especially with previous caesarean + anterior praevia)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Colour Doppler** if vasa praevia suspected (see pulsation on transducer)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - NICE definitions: **Minor (edge >20mm from os)**; **Major (covers os)**
  - **Low-lying placenta:** placental edge <20mm from internal os (not praevia, but managed similarly)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent transvaginal ultrasound** to confirm placental relationship to os — if you omit this, you may miss a minor praevia and attempt a vaginal delivery, leading to massive haemorrhage.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess severity:** haemodynamic status (pulse, BP, shock index), blood loss (visible + concealed)
  - **IV access:** two large-bore cannulae (16G minimum)
  - **Cross-match:** send for 4 units of packed cells (or massive transfusion protocol if unstable)
  - **Call senior:** obstetric registrar and consultant immediately — they will decide on need for emergency caesarean section (LSCS)
  - **Lie patient flat/in left lateral tilt** (avoid supine hypotension)
  - **Start IV fluids** if bleeding but give only enough to maintain BP (permissive hypotension until blood available)
  - **Fetal monitoring:** continuous CTG if >24 weeks
  - **Anti-D** if Rh-negative and no sensitisation (give within 72 hours of any bleed)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Admit** all patients with suspected or confirmed praevia after first bleed – even if bleeding stops
  - **Continue CTG monitoring** (at least 2-hourly if stable, continuous if ongoing bleeding)
  - **Administer corticosteroids** for lung maturity if <36 weeks – see Drugs & Doses
  - **Magnesium sulfate** for neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks – check local protocol for dose
  - **Regular maternal obs** (q4h initially, then q8h if stable)
  - **Plan elective LSCS** – timing depends on praevia type and bleeding:
    - Major praevia: 36-37 weeks (NICE)
    - Minor praevia: 38-39 weeks
    - Emergency LSCS if recurrent heavy bleed at any gestation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If stable after first bleed, discharge only if:
    - Minor praevia + no further bleeding + fetal head engaged (unlikely)
    - Strict follow-up plan with serial scans
  - Usually remains admitted until delivery (especially major praevia)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV Hartmann's or normal saline, 500ml bolus PRN for hypotension (then switch to blood)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** TED stockings only (avoid LMWH if active bleeding or high risk of delivery)
  - **Diet/NBM status:** generally eat normally unless signs of labour/planned LSCS
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly vitals (BP, HR, temperature, respiratory rate) plus daily CTG
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Corticosteroids (betamethasone):**
  - Betamethasone 11.4mg IM (as betamethasone sodium phosphate + acetate) *or* dexamethasone 12mg IM? **The standard UK regime**: Betamethasone 12mg IM (3.5ml of 3.3mg/ml) given as two doses 24 hours apart. *[Check local policy for exact preparation]*
- **Anti-D immunoglobulin (Rh-negative, non-sensitised):**
  - Dose: 250 IU (for <20 weeks) or 500 IU (for >20 weeks) IM within 72 hours of any antepartum bleed. *[Check local policy for exact dose and product]*
- **Tocolysis:** **Not recommended** for placenta praevia due to risk of exacerbating haemorrhage. Avoid nifedipine, atosiban, or terbutaline.
- **Magnesium sulfate (for neuroprotection):**
  - 4g IV bolus over 20-30 min, then 1g/hour for 24 hours (if delivery <30 weeks planned). *[Check local policy]*
- **Common alternatives:** None for specific praevia management.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid vaginal progesterone (may increase bleeding risk).
  - Avoid NSAIDs for pain (risk of bleeding + premature ductus closure if delivery imminent).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None of the above are significantly renally cleared.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving corticosteroids as a single large IM dose (e.g. 12mg x1) instead of two doses.** This is common. The course must be two doses 24 hours apart for maximum lung benefit. Missing the second dose reduces efficacy. Also: **never give IV corticosteroids unless ordered** – betamethasone IM only.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Massive haemorrhage leading to maternal shock, DIC, death
  - Fetal anaemia, hypoxia, stillbirth
  - Fetal bradycardia from vasa praevia rupture
  - Preterm labour and delivery (iatrogenic or spontaneous)
- **Chronic:**
  - Placenta accreta/increta/percreta (especially with previous caesarean scar) – leads to morbidly adherent placenta, massive transfusion, possible hysterectomy
  - Recurrent admissions, maternal anxiety
  - Fetal growth restriction (if coexisting)
sections.Prognosis: - Maternal mortality <1% in high-resource settings with timely management; fetal mortality 10-15% if presentation after 24 weeks (preterm delivery). With elective LSCS at 36-38 weeks, fetal outcome is generally good (92-97% survival). Risk of neonatal respiratory distress if delivered before corticosteroids completed.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always have an ultrasound report documenting placental distance from os in any woman >20 weeks with bleeding** – if unclear, get a transvaginal scan.
- **If you must perform a speculum exam, do it gently with ultrasound confirmation of praevia first** – or call senior to decide.
- **Document fetal presentation** – praevia often coexists with malpresentation; plan LSCS if breech.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never perform a digital vaginal exam** in a woman with undifferentiated APH – you can cause torrential haemorrhage.
- **Don't send a stable patient home after first minor bleed** without discussing with registrar – most will be admitted for observation and steroids.
- **Don't forget Kleihauer and anti-D in Rh-negative women** – sensitisation can affect future pregnancies.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Any new or increased vaginal bleeding (even spotting)
  - Abdominal pain or contractions
  - Reduced fetal movements
  - Leakage of fluid (ruptured membranes)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Obstetric clinic within 1 week for repeat ultrasound and plan
  - Arrange anti-D follow-up if still Rh-negative
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call obstetric registrar immediately if:**
  - Haemodynamically unstable (tachycardia >110 bpm, systolic BP <90)
  - Visible heavy bleeding (soaking pad every 10-15 min)
  - CTG changes (reduced variability, late decelerations, bradycardia)
  - Suspected vasa praevia (fetal distress + painless bleed)
- **Call consultant / anaesthetist + prepare for emergency LSCS if:**
  - Shock, gross haemorrhage, or NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L
  - Fetal bradycardia <100 bpm for 3 min
  - Maternal GCS <15 or altered consciousness

rawMarkdown:
# Placenta Praevia
**One-liner:** Placenta partially or completely overlies internal cervical os after 20 weeks; painless bleeding in third trimester is the classic presentation; risk of catastrophic maternal haemorrhage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive antepartum haemorrhage (APH) with shock:** The bleeding is unprovoked, bright red, and painless. The single finding: **tachycardia >110 bpm with postural hypotension** despite a "small" amount of visible blood — the concealed haemorrhage can be larger than it appears.
- **Vasa praevia:** Fetal vessels running across the membranes over the os. Presents with sudden, painless bleeding *and* **fetal bradycardia <100 bpm** — the baby bleeds from its own circulation. A normal maternal pulse and blood pressure with a distressed fetus should raise this immediately.
- **Placental abruption with concealed haemorrhage:** Painful, hard "woody" uterus, often with fetal distress. **Uterine hypertonus on palpation** differentiates it from praevia (soft uterus). If you see a rigid uterus + fetal bradycardia, think abruption first.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - **Painless, bright red vaginal bleeding** (classic – 90% of cases) — usually after 28 weeks, may be recurrent or single episode
  - Sometimes only spotting or brown discharge (low-grade bleed)
  - May be provoked by sexual intercourse or vaginal exam (massively increases bleeding risk)
  - Bleeding can be torrential or intermittent
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Uterus is **soft and non-tender** (differentiates from abruption)
  - Fetal lie often **breech or transverse** due to placenta occupying lower segment
  - If bleeding heavily: maternal tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, clammy skin
  - **Avoid digital vaginal exam** – this can precipitate catastrophic haemorrhage; only speculum exam under ultrasound guidance after praevia excluded

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Placental abruption** — painful, hard uterus, concealed bleed, fetal distress out of proportion to visible blood
- **Vasa praevia** — painless bleeding + fetal bradycardia, maternal vitals stable; diagnose with colour Doppler ultrasound
- **Cervical/ectropion** — bleeding after intercourse/exam, no fetal distress, ultrasound normal
- **Cervical/vulval trauma** — history of trauma, localised bleeding
- **Preterm labour** — contractions with show, not pure fresh bleeding
- **Lower genital tract infection** — discharge with blood streaks, not heavy
- **Splitter:** **Pain on palpation of the uterus** → abruption (hard, tender) vs praevia (soft, non-tender) — this is the most reliable bedside differentiator.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Fetal heart auscultation** or **CTG** — look for bradycardia, decelerations (fetal distress)
  - **Speculum exam** (gentle) — visualise cervical os: is bleeding coming through os? Avoid digital exam.
  - **Ultrasound (urgent):** confirm placental location, measure distance from internal os; transvaginal is safe and accurate (no harm distal to os)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, platelets), group & screen (cross-match if bleeding)
  - Clotting screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen) — DIC can develop rapidly with large bleed
  - Kleihauer test (if Rh-negative, to calculate anti-D dose)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transabdominal ultrasound (screening); if equivocal, transvaginal (gold standard)
  - **Second-line:** MRI if suspected placenta accreta (especially with previous caesarean + anterior praevia)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Colour Doppler** if vasa praevia suspected (see pulsation on transducer)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - NICE definitions: **Minor (edge >20mm from os)**; **Major (covers os)**
  - **Low-lying placenta:** placental edge <20mm from internal os (not praevia, but managed similarly)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent transvaginal ultrasound** to confirm placental relationship to os — if you omit this, you may miss a minor praevia and attempt a vaginal delivery, leading to massive haemorrhage.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess severity:** haemodynamic status (pulse, BP, shock index), blood loss (visible + concealed)
  - **IV access:** two large-bore cannulae (16G minimum)
  - **Cross-match:** send for 4 units of packed cells (or massive transfusion protocol if unstable)
  - **Call senior:** obstetric registrar and consultant immediately — they will decide on need for emergency caesarean section (LSCS)
  - **Lie patient flat/in left lateral tilt** (avoid supine hypotension)
  - **Start IV fluids** if bleeding but give only enough to maintain BP (permissive hypotension until blood available)
  - **Fetal monitoring:** continuous CTG if >24 weeks
  - **Anti-D** if Rh-negative and no sensitisation (give within 72 hours of any bleed)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Admit** all patients with suspected or confirmed praevia after first bleed – even if bleeding stops
  - **Continue CTG monitoring** (at least 2-hourly if stable, continuous if ongoing bleeding)
  - **Administer corticosteroids** for lung maturity if <36 weeks – see Drugs & Doses
  - **Magnesium sulfate** for neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks – check local protocol for dose
  - **Regular maternal obs** (q4h initially, then q8h if stable)
  - **Plan elective LSCS** – timing depends on praevia type and bleeding:
    - Major praevia: 36-37 weeks (NICE)
    - Minor praevia: 38-39 weeks
    - Emergency LSCS if recurrent heavy bleed at any gestation
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If stable after first bleed, discharge only if:
    - Minor praevia + no further bleeding + fetal head engaged (unlikely)
    - Strict follow-up plan with serial scans
  - Usually remains admitted until delivery (especially major praevia)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV Hartmann's or normal saline, 500ml bolus PRN for hypotension (then switch to blood)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** TED stockings only (avoid LMWH if active bleeding or high risk of delivery)
  - **Diet/NBM status:** generally eat normally unless signs of labour/planned LSCS
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly vitals (BP, HR, temperature, respiratory rate) plus daily CTG

### Drugs & Doses
- **Corticosteroids (betamethasone):**
  - Betamethasone 11.4mg IM (as betamethasone sodium phosphate + acetate) *or* dexamethasone 12mg IM? **The standard UK regime**: Betamethasone 12mg IM (3.5ml of 3.3mg/ml) given as two doses 24 hours apart. *[Check local policy for exact preparation]*
- **Anti-D immunoglobulin (Rh-negative, non-sensitised):**
  - Dose: 250 IU (for <20 weeks) or 500 IU (for >20 weeks) IM within 72 hours of any antepartum bleed. *[Check local policy for exact dose and product]*
- **Tocolysis:** **Not recommended** for placenta praevia due to risk of exacerbating haemorrhage. Avoid nifedipine, atosiban, or terbutaline.
- **Magnesium sulfate (for neuroprotection):**
  - 4g IV bolus over 20-30 min, then 1g/hour for 24 hours (if delivery <30 weeks planned). *[Check local policy]*
- **Common alternatives:** None for specific praevia management.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid vaginal progesterone (may increase bleeding risk).
  - Avoid NSAIDs for pain (risk of bleeding + premature ductus closure if delivery imminent).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None of the above are significantly renally cleared.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving corticosteroids as a single large IM dose (e.g. 12mg x1) instead of two doses.** This is common. The course must be two doses 24 hours apart for maximum lung benefit. Missing the second dose reduces efficacy. Also: **never give IV corticosteroids unless ordered** – betamethasone IM only.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Massive haemorrhage leading to maternal shock, DIC, death
  - Fetal anaemia, hypoxia, stillbirth
  - Fetal bradycardia from vasa praevia rupture
  - Preterm labour and delivery (iatrogenic or spontaneous)
- **Chronic:**
  - Placenta accreta/increta/percreta (especially with previous caesarean scar) – leads to morbidly adherent placenta, massive transfusion, possible hysterectomy
  - Recurrent admissions, maternal anxiety
  - Fetal growth restriction (if coexisting)

### Prognosis
- Maternal mortality <1% in high-resource settings with timely management; fetal mortality 10-15% if presentation after 24 weeks (preterm delivery). With elective LSCS at 36-38 weeks, fetal outcome is generally good (92-97% survival). Risk of neonatal respiratory distress if delivered before corticosteroids completed.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always have an ultrasound report documenting placental distance from os in any woman >20 weeks with bleeding** – if unclear, get a transvaginal scan.
- **If you must perform a speculum exam, do it gently with ultrasound confirmation of praevia first** – or call senior to decide.
- **Document fetal presentation** – praevia often coexists with malpresentation; plan LSCS if breech.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never perform a digital vaginal exam** in a woman with undifferentiated APH – you can cause torrential haemorrhage.
- **Don't send a stable patient home after first minor bleed** without discussing with registrar – most will be admitted for observation and steroids.
- **Don't forget Kleihauer and anti-D in Rh-negative women** – sensitisation can affect future pregnancies.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Any new or increased vaginal bleeding (even spotting)
  - Abdominal pain or contractions
  - Reduced fetal movements
  - Leakage of fluid (ruptured membranes)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Obstetric clinic within 1 week for repeat ultrasound and plan
  - Arrange anti-D follow-up if still Rh-negative

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call obstetric registrar immediately if:**
  - Haemodynamically unstable (tachycardia >110 bpm, systolic BP <90)
  - Visible heavy bleeding (soaking pad every 10-15 min)
  - CTG changes (reduced variability, late decelerations, bradycardia)
  - Suspected vasa praevia (fetal distress + painless bleed)
- **Call consultant / anaesthetist + prepare for emergency LSCS if:**
  - Shock, gross haemorrhage, or NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L
  - Fetal bradycardia <100 bpm for 3 min
  - Maternal GCS <15 or altered consciousness
